page: Amazon Web Services r8in.4xlarge is a memory-optimized instance built on the x86_64 architecture. It features 16 dedicated virtual CPUs powered by an Intel processor running at a clock speed of 3.9 GHz. The underlying hardware allocation provides 8 physical cores with 2 threads per core, managed via the AWS Nitro hypervisor.

This instance is configured with 128.0 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core. It does not include local NVMe storage or integrated GPU hardware, meaning all storage requirements must be met through network-attached Elastic Block Store volumes.

Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 25 Gbps, optimized for network and EBS throughput. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the r8in.4xlarge offers a dense memory profile relative to its compute resources, making it a viable option for memory-bound workloads that do not require local physical disks or hardware acceleration.

The resource configuration of the r8in.4xlarge is designed for memory-intensive applications. Appropriate workloads include relational and non-relational databases, in-memory caching layers, enterprise applications, and data analytics pipelines that require substantial RAM but moderate CPU capacity.
description: Amazon Web Services r8in.4xlarge is an x86_64 memory-optimized instance featuring 16 dedicated vCPUs and 128.0 GB of RAM. Powered by an Intel processor running at 3.9 GHz, this instance utilizes the AWS Nitro hypervisor to deliver dedicated compute resources with 8 physical cores and 2 threads per core. The instance lacks local storage and GPU accelerators, relying entirely on network-attached storage. It provides a baseline network bandwidth of 25 Gbps, optimized for network and EBS performance. With a high memory-to-vCPU ratio of 8.0 GB per core, the r8in.4xlarge is structured for memory-intensive workloads. It is qualitatively cost-efficient for applications requiring significant memory capacity without the overhead of local physical disks. Suitable workloads include in-memory databases, caching systems, and enterprise applications.
